evidence_quotes.quote: INFLUENCER MARKETING TRADE BODY

paragraphs.text: IMTB INFLUENCER MARKETING TRADE BODY Ref. Ares(2025)9122558 - 24/10/2025
Digital Fairness Act consultation

section_path: Feedback to the call for evidence submitted by the Influencer Marketing Trade Body

paragraphs.text: 24 October 2025

section_path: Feedback to the call for evidence submitted by the Influencer Marketing Trade Body

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing industry

paragraphs.text: The Influencer Marketing Trade Body (IMTB) is a not-for-profit trade association dedicated to building a robust, sustainable future for the influencer marketing industry through increased accountability, governance and a unified voice.

section_path: About the Influencer Marketing Trade Body

evidence_quotes.quote: Influencer marketing is, by nature transnational, many of our members' influencer campaigns focus on consumers across Europe.

paragraphs.text: Launched in October 2021 with six founding members, the IMTB now counts circa. 30 progressive creator economy companies as members. We are UK based, though many of our members have offices across Europe. Influencer marketing is, by nature transnational, many of our members' influencer campaigns focus on consumers across Europe.

section_path: About the Influencer Marketing Trade Body

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing

paragraphs.text: IMTB represents influencer marketing on the Committee of Advertising Practice ("CAP"), the UK advertising's rule-making body.

section_path: About the Influencer Marketing Trade Body

evidence_quotes.quote: It co-owns the Influencer Marketing Code of Conduct alongside ISBA.

paragraphs.text: It co-owns the Influencer Marketing Code of Conduct alongside ISBA. The Code aims to standardise good practices and guidance with brands, agencies and creators. It covers areas including disclosure, transparency, pay parity, and effective representation.

section_path: About the Influencer Marketing Trade Body

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing workstream

paragraphs.text: IMTB sits on the UK Government's Online Advertising Taskforce as part of its influencer marketing workstream.

section_path: About the Influencer Marketing Trade Body

paragraphs.text: Influencer Marketing Trade Body Limited. Company number 1324759 Registered address at Level 3 207 Regent Street, London, United Kingdom, W1B 3HH.

section_path: About the Influencer Marketing Trade Body

paragraphs.text: IMTB is co-secretariat to the Digital Creators All-Party Parliamentary Group, designed as a forum for creators to engage policymakers. It acts as a vital bridge between the UK's creators and Westminster

section_path: About the Influencer Marketing Trade Body

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing associations

paragraphs.text: In July 2024 IMTB co-founded the European Influencer Marketing Alliance (EIMA) alongside influencer marketing associations in France (UMICC) and Germany (BVIM). EIMA provides a unified voice for creator economy constituents to regulators, legislators, media, academics and other key stakeholders at a time of tremendous growth within the industry but also a time of increased legislative and regulatory scrutiny.

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing bodies

paragraphs.text: Today EIMA represents 300 agencies and 8,000 creators through influencer marketing bodies in seven countries:

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

paragraphs.text: France, UMICC – Union des Métiers de l'Influence et des Créateurs de Contenu

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

evidence_quotes.quote: Influencer Marketing

paragraphs.text: Germany, BVIM – Bundesverband Influencer Marketing

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

evidence_quotes.quote: Influencer Marketing Trade Body

paragraphs.text: United Kingdom, IMTB – Influencer Marketing Trade Body

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

evidence_quotes.quote: Influencer Marketing Association

paragraphs.text: Belgium, IMA – Influencer Marketing Association

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

paragraphs.text: Spain, APIDE – Asociación Profesional de Influencers y Digitales de España Italy, AssoInfluencer

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

paragraphs.text: Poland, SPIM – Stowarzyszenie Polskiego Influencer Marketingu).

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

evidence_quotes.quote: support for influencers as online content creators.

paragraphs.text: EIMA's expansion is in line with recommendations from the European Union Council's publication of conclusions on support for influencers as online content creators. Specifically to:

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

evidence_quotes.quote: raise awareness among influencers about their role in the media ecosystem and the impact they have on their audience
self-regulatory bodies or mechanisms, including a possible ethical code or similar initiatives for influencers

paragraphs.text: a. "Support emerging representative organisations to systematically raise awareness among influencers about their role in the media ecosystem and the impact they have on their audience" [see para. 29]; b. "Encourage and support, in relevant ways, emerging representative organisations, influencer agencies, brands and other relevant actors in the development of self-regulatory bodies or mechanisms, including a possible ethical code or similar initiatives for influencers" [see para 37];

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

evidence_quotes.quote: the influencer training and certification programme

paragraphs.text: In July 2024 EIMA became an EASA member. It endorses EASA's initiative adEthics - the influencer training and certification programme available in 10 countries.

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance

evidence_quotes.quote: existing legal framework for influencers, influencers marketers and advertisers

paragraphs.text: IMTB applauds the objectives of a potential Digital Fairness Act (DFA). However, it believes these aims could be achieved by building greater awareness of responsibilities as laid out within the existing legal framework for influencers, influencers marketers and advertisers and achieving higher compliance rates through education with a hard backstop provided by existing legal framework.

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance
Comments and recommendations

evidence_quotes.quote: European Influencer Marketing Alliance (EIMA)

paragraphs.text: Education and compliance are already being driven by industry actors including the European Influencer Marketing Alliance (EIMA), of which IMTB is a co-founder, and the European Advertising Standards Alliance (EASA), of which EIMA is a member.

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance
Comments and recommendations

evidence_quotes.quote: influencer marketing practices

paragraphs.text: The existing legal framework already covers influencer marketing practices effectively, including addressing hidden advertising and problematic content. For example:

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance
Comments and recommendations

evidence_quotes.quote: Non-disclosure / hidden advertising is banned in EU law under the AVMSD (Article 9(1)), UCPD (Article 7(2), and DSA (Article 26 DSA).

paragraphs.text: Non-disclosure / hidden advertising is banned in EU law under the AVMSD (Article 9(1)), UCPD (Article 7(2), and DSA (Article 26 DSA).

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance
Comments and recommendations

evidence_quotes.quote: The UCPD additionally provides strong tools to address deceptive practices and online fraud, including fake likes and followers.

paragraphs.text: The UCPD additionally provides strong tools to address deceptive practices and online fraud, including fake likes and followers. This is supplemented by the Commission's UCPD guidance with a dedicated section on "online marketing".

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance
Comments and recommendations

evidence_quotes.quote: audiovisual commercial communication shall be readily recognisable as such

paragraphs.text: Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AMSD) (Directive 2010/13/EU) Art. 28b(2) in conjunction with Art. 9(1)(a) states that audiovisual commercial communication shall be readily recognisable as such

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance
Comments and recommendations

evidence_quotes.quote: The DSA requires online platforms to take action against illegal content and products, including scams, by removing them or restricting access.

paragraphs.text: The DSA requires online platforms to take action against illegal content and products, including scams, by removing them or restricting access.

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance
Comments and recommendations

evidence_quotes.quote: misleading advertising

paragraphs.text: The Misleading and Comparative Advertising Directive protects consumers and traders from misleading advertising. It prohibits claims that are false, ambiguous, or deceptive.

section_path: About the European Influencer Marketing Alliance
Comments and recommendations

evidence_quotes.quote: IMTB promotes responsible influencer marketing - marketing that can be trusted by consumers, trusted by advertisers and trusted by regulators.

paragraphs.text: IMTB promotes responsible influencer marketing - marketing that can be trusted by consumers, trusted by advertisers and trusted by regulators. IMTB promotes self-regulation and co-regulation initiatives led by professional organizations. EIMA and its members already promote codes of ethics, educational initiatives, and enforcement mechanisms. EU-level recognition of these efforts would foster credibility and uptake.

section_path: Influencer marketing self regulation enhances statutory regulation

evidence_quotes.quote: Through EIMA, IMTB believes there is an opportunity for regulators, SROs, trade associations and social media platforms to work in concert to create a wider and deeper sweep of influencer content in terms of numbers of influencers analysed, platforms content published to and the verticals in which they are published.
The exercise would build on the 2023 sweep of 576 influencers and would chart progress towards acceptable disclosure levels.

paragraphs.text: Through EIMA, IMTB believes there is an opportunity for regulators, SROs, trade associations and social media platforms to work in concert to create a wider and deeper sweep of influencer content in terms of numbers of influencers analysed, platforms content published to and the verticals in which they are published. The exercise would build on the 2023 sweep of 576 influencers and would chart progress towards acceptable disclosure levels.

section_path: Influencer marketing self regulation enhances statutory regulation

evidence_quotes.quote: Social media platforms might be encouraged to promote the Influencer Legal Hub to influencers using its platform.

paragraphs.text: The excellent work undertaken on the Influencer Legal Hub might be built upon to create bite sized content delivered in the style and form most likely to be absorbed by influencers. Social media platforms might be encouraged to promote the Influencer Legal Hub to influencers using its platform.

section_path: Influencer marketing self regulation enhances statutory regulation

paragraphs.text: Introducing new regulation in addition to the existing legal framework risks duplicating laws and confusing consumers and business. It comes at a time when European nations are calling for "self-restraint when it comes to new legislation."9

section_path: Simplification

evidence_quotes.quote: IMTB would caution against the passing of new influencer marketing-specific laws.
Instead, all actors operating within the influencer marketing ecosystem should comply with existing advertising regulations like any other form of advertising.

paragraphs.text: IMTB would caution against the passing of new influencer marketing-specific laws. To do so would ghetto-ize our sector. Instead, all actors operating within the influencer marketing ecosystem should comply with existing advertising regulations like any other form of advertising.

section_path: Simplification

paragraphs.text: Effective self-regulation is designed to complement regulation. It offers a faster and less expensive way to respond to issues that spring up in society compared with statutory regulation. Implementing and enforcing advertising standards are carried by the local ad ecosystem.

section_path: Simplification

paragraphs.text: The harmonisation of international standards, through recognition of the International Chamber of Commerce (ICC) Advertising and Marketing

section_path: Simplification

paragraphs.text: Communications Code, helps reduce non-tariff barriers to trade and facilitates the free flow of goods and services.
